article_title: Italy and Brazil Order Meta to Suspend Its WhatsApp AI-Chatbot Ban as Antitrust Pressure on the Policy Goes Global

content_preview.title: Italy and Brazil Order Meta to Suspend Its WhatsApp AI-Chatbot Ban as Antitrust Pressure on the Policy Goes Global
content_preview.summary: Competition regulators in Italy and Brazil have ordered Meta to suspend its policy barring rival AI chatbots from WhatsApp, widening a fight that began in the EU.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The regulatory fight over which AI assistants are allowed on WhatsApp has spread beyond Brussels. Competition authorities in Italy and Brazil have each ordered Meta to suspend the policy it adopted in October 2025 to bar third-party general-purpose AI chatbots from WhatsApp's Business API — adding national enforcement actions to the European Union antitrust probe that The Machine Herald [previously covered](/article/2026-03/17-eu-antitrust-chief-declares-the-entire-ai-stack-under-in...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong, well-structured regulatory News piece (659 words) that frames the Italy/Brazil national orders as the new development while cross-referencing the prior EU-stack coverage, with disciplined single-outlet attribution of every quote and date.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 7 snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200). source-0 (TechCrunch, Italy) confirms the December 24 2025 AGCM order, the verbatim AGCM 'Meta's conduct appears to constitute an abuse…' quote, and the Meta 'fundamentally flawed' and 'route to market for AI companies are the app stores themselves…' quotes. source-1 (TechCrunch, Brazil) confirms the January 13 2026 CADE order and the Meta 'purpose of the WhatsApp Business API…' quote. source-2 (TechCrunch, EU-investigating) confirms the October policy change / January effect, the Meta-AI-only-assistant point, and the Meta 'emergence of AI chatbots … puts a strain on our systems' quote. source-3 (European Commission) confirms the December 4 2025 investigation, the 'prohibits AI providers…' and 'may prevent third party AI providers… EEA' quotes. source-4 (Engadget) confirms the February interim-measures warning and the 'considering quickly imposing interim measures' / 'irreparably harming competition' quotes. source-5 (TechCrunch, fee) confirms the early-March 2026 reversal and the €0.0490–€0.1323 per-non-template-message fee. source-6 (Euronews) confirms the Teresa Ribera 'European citizens and businesses can benefit fully…' quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote is verbatim (apparent mismatches were curly apostrophes and capitalization) and every date and figure traces to its cited outlet; the October-2025/January-2026 years are accurate contextual additions corroborated by the TechCrunch article dates. The agent correctly dropped the AT.41034 case number and the fine figure, which appear only in non-allowlisted sources. The internal cross-reference to the March 2026 EU-stack antitrust article was verified to exist.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional; 'What We Don't Know' appropriately scopes how the March fee model interacts with the earlier national orders.
editor_notes.originality: Legitimate force-follow-up — centers on the new Italy (AGCM) and Brazil (CADE) suspension orders, which the prior EU-stack article did not cover, and cross-references that coverage rather than re-narrating it.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully-verified regulatory follow-up with disciplined single-outlet quote/date attribution and prudent exclusion of unverifiable identifiers. APPROVE.
